Welcome to the Gary Helbert Letter All-Star Audio series. I'm Kevin Rogers. The first lesson every copywriter learns is the importance of a powerful headline. And in this recording you're going to get Gary's best insider tips and secrets for writing great ones. And who better to present it than the legendary Magalog master copywriter David Deutsch. David is frequently referred to as one of the top direct response marketing copywriters in the world. And he's penned many direct mail controls for major publishers such as Boardroom and Rodale. He's also the author of Think Inside the Box and a claimed business creativity book that reveals how to generate money-making ideas. Deutsch started his career at David Ogilvy's renowned Ogilvy and Mather Agency on New York's Madison Avenue. And it was soon after leaving there that he was introduced to Gary Helbert. A mutual friend suggested that Gary invite David to be on the faculty of one of his famous Key West seminars. So as David tells it, one day I get a call from Gary and I'm in shock and struck by the fact that he talks exactly like he writes. The same cadences, vocabulary, syntax. My wife came with me to that first Key West seminar and the famous guru she had heard me singing the praises of for years made his appearance in shorts, a scuzzy t-shirt and a cap that read, clients suck. David talks about his fondest memory, wandering around Times Square with Gary late one night after a wonderfully New York dinner at Carnegie Deli. I saw and appreciated and enjoyed my native city in a whole new way that night, fueled by Gary's gusto and joy de vivre. So here now is the great David Deutsch reading How to Write Killer Headlines. Hi, this is David Deutsch and I'm really pleased today to be here to read and go over an issue of the Gary Helbert letter with you. Gary means a lot to me as a, he was a friend, he was a mentor, he was a great influence on my copywriting. I met him when I was just starting out as a freelancer and he invited me to his seminars down in Key West beyond faculty and had a chance to work with him, see him in action. I've studied his writings and his teachings for many years and really learned from him not just about copywriting but about marketing, about selling, about the mindset that you've got to have when you market and sell, how you have to be aware of the fact that you're selling at all times. I have to keep the leader interested at all times and entertaining and engaging and he was just such a great example of that. Taught me about breaking limits, preconceived notions, getting through preconceived notions and even taught me about living fully. He lived with such zest and such joy that that was an inspiration to me as well. Each issue of the Gary Helbert letter I think should be read at least three times. One to just read it, enjoy it, see the effect it has on you. The other is for the information and advice that it contains and then lastly just to see really how he does what he does, to see how he uses the copywriting techniques that he talks about, to see how he uses the marketing techniques and to see how he uses the self-promotional techniques to promote himself which he does so incredibly well. So now I'm going to read to you an issue of the Gary Helbert letter and here's how it starts out. From Ray West of Jewfish Creek, dear friend and subscriber, my head hurts, my feet stink and I don't love Jimmy Swagger. I don't want to be here at my desk writing this letter. I want to be on my boat. Writing sucks. I hate to write. Anybody who says they like to write is either a degenerate liar or a social leper. Let's face it, what kind of word wants to sit and struggle with a blank piece of paper when he could be 60 feet underwater slaughtering innocent fish or reminiscing his honey or going to a movie or playing golf or reading what somebody else has struggled to write or anything, anything but writing. But alas, judging from the calls on my electronic mailbox, the numero uno subject my subscribers want me to write about is writing. So I am. Now the first thing I want you to know about writing other than how much it sucks is the best way to get good at writing is by writing. I know that sounds like double talk but it isn't. The biggest secret to writing well is simply work. Just the act of doing it. For the moment, forget about getting it perfect. Forget about grammar, forget about syntax, punctuation and all that. Just sit down and start writing. John D. McDonald was one of the best writers of all time. He died in December of 1986 but fortunately he left the legacy of some of the most memorable fiction ever penned. In fact, I became literally addicted to his Travis McGee series. You will too once you read one of them. Anyway, when he was first getting started he read somewhere that the average novelist didn't really hit his stride until he had written about 10 books. And so what do you think McDonald did? Very simple. He wrote the equivalent of 10 novels in one year. Listen, writing does not get easier. However, starting to write does. Therefore, if you've got a job of writing to do, just sit down and start. Don't wait to get organized. Don't wait for ideas or inspiration. Don't wait until you feel like it. I never do. Just start. Okay, enough of that. Now it goes without saying but let's say it anyway. Kind of writing we're concerned with here is the kind that convinces people to buy something and that's the hardest kind of writing of all. It's also the most profitable. You know, I routinely get more for writing a single letter than most authors get for writing an entire book or sometimes 10 entire books. So let's get specific. To sell somebody something you've got to do four things. One, you've got to catch their attention. Two, you've got to capture their interest. Three, you've got to arouse their desire. Sounds dirty, doesn't it? And four, you've got to motivate them to action. This, as you may know, is called the AIDA or ADA formula. A attention, I interest, D desire, A action. Today, we're going to concentrate on getting people's attention by learning how to write what I call killer headlines. Hark unto me. Listen, if you want to capture the attention of someone in a crowd, what's the best way to do it? It's really quite elementary, my dear Watson. What you do is you call him by name and if you want someone riveted to your sales letters, hook up with some outfit that can do ad mark or laser printing or else use your word processor and put your leader's name in a headline printed at the top of the sales letter you sent him. Like this. Then swallow his attempt suicide after realizing he'll never be as good looking as Gary Halbert. Or, Burbanker fails to hire the Prince of Print and loses millions. Or, Frank K. Wood cashes in big by mailing unique concept letter developed by California Madman. Or, Jimmy Colano changes his will and leaves all his millions to guru Gary. Or, Alicia Evans expresses astonishment at seeing her name in newsletter written by demented nerd from Ohio and so on. What I've just described is the ultimate way to get attention in a sales letter. No one, not even someone as blasé or steamy griffin, will fail to perk up at seeing his or her name in print, especially if it's unexpected. Of course, we can't always use our prospects names in our headlines. So, what are some other good ideas? Here are three of the most powerful. Put news in your headline. Promise a benefit in your headline. To both of the above in the same headline. Here are some good words to use in headlines. Announcing at last, now, now at last. How to. Here are 17 ways to. The art of, the secret of, a startling fact about, amazing, new, and so on. Now, here's a headline that combines both news value and the promise of a benefit. At last, scientists discover new ways to look younger in just 17 days. There's so much to be learned in the 13 words above. At last, boy, that sure suggests something. We've been waiting for has finally happened, hasn't it? Goodie, goodie. Scientists. Yeah, not just nobody. Not some no nothing bag lady living in the streets. Not some ad man or copyrighter. No, no, no. We're talking about a select, objective, hard headed, analytical, totally honest group of folks who validate our headlines. Discover. Wow. It seems to connote research laboratories and maybe a scientific breakthrough. Doesn't it? New way. Who's our who's our? We're all questing for new ways continually, aren't we? New ways to grow hair, get thin, make more money, etc. To look younger. Who among us is there over the age of 30 that wouldn't welcome the achieving of this almost universally sought after benefit in just 17 days? More validation because it's so specific. Grothop can set it. Generalities fall off your leaders like water off a duck's back or something like that. Anyway, the specificity of 17 days makes our headline more believable, more easily understood and somehow more interesting and more real. How important are headlines? I'm glad you asked. Sometimes it's say the headline accounts for 80% of the success of the ad. I myself have re-headlined ads and increased their poll by 475%. I have a client who pays me $195,000 per year to write headlines. Headlines are where I spend more creative effort than any other aspect of my work. Good headlines are crucial. Let's take a breather so I can ask you a question. What publication has the highest paid writers? Answer. The National Enquirer. Yep, it's true. And for the reason, you see, the writers for the National Enquirer are among the best anywhere on earth. I'm not being sarcastic. They really are. If you doubt that, here's something for you to consider. More people read any single issue of the Enquirer than have read the Bible since it was first printed. Enquirer articles are superbly written. They're clear, concise, crisp, and all in all, the most easily understood articles of any publication. And what do Enquirer writers excel at above all else? You got it. Headlines. Their headlines are so powerful, they have so much grabbing power that every week, people who have sworn they'll never again by such a publication are almost forced to purchase it in spite of themselves. So anyway, if the best way to get good at writing is by writing, what is the best way to get good at writing headlines? That's easy. It's by writing headlines. Here's what you do. Every week, you get copies of the National Enquirer, Star, Globe, Weekly Road News, and so on. And on a monthly basis, you get copies of Readestigist, Cosmopolitan, and any other magazine you can find that has lots of good headlines. And you get yourself a large supply of 3x5 index cards, and you write all those good headlines on cards, one to a card. By the way, a superb source of good headlines is one of the reference reports included in my seminar tapes. It's called, 349 Great Headlines, 152 Attention Grabber Words, Sample Ads, Sample Letters. This is truly a masterpiece collection of headlines and other ammo that should be part of every copywriter's library. Many of you, perhaps most, have my seminar tapes and all the related materials. And those of you who don't should. Call an order right now, 323-851-8275. And all this valuable info will be on its way to you in a matter of hours. Forgive me, pitching is in my bones, and I just can't help myself sometimes, especially if I have a strong belief in the product. Whatever. Okay, now we've got all these hundreds of headlines on hundreds of headline cards, and in a moment, I'm going to show you how to massage all this good stuff to produce maximum results. But first, a true story. Remember that ad I sent with my newsletter a couple of months back? The one where I said, when it comes to advertising, I was the best thing since sliced bread, and that to get me, you had to shell out $15,000 up front and offer me substantial carrot, usually 5% of sales on the back end. All right, I got a call as a result of that ad from a guy in Denver who says he would have no problem paying me 15,000 advance, but the 5% part wouldn't work since he's a furniture dealer, three stores, and furniture is sold at varying markups. So I come back to him by asking what I'd have to do for him to be worth $15,000 per month. I release my business by 25%, he says, send me the $15,000 advance and 15,000 more on the first of every month, and I'll feed to it that you sell every stick of furniture possible to sell in Denver, I say. He did, and I did. Here's the way we were. First, you need to know that I now have these people on a schedule of 365 full-page newspaper ads per year. In a week or so, that schedule will be bumped to about 400 pages per year, and soon it will be 730 pages per year, and later, when we hit 1,000 pages per year, we're going to slag off. By the way, all this is supplemented with a steady stream of radio advertising and a little TV. Now, there's no way in the world I'm going to commit to writing hundreds of pages per year, and besides, I'll never know the furniture business as well as my client. So the way we work is, I theme the thrust of the advertising, and I write the headlines to go with these themes. Then, my client writes the ad and faxes them to me, and I read them and make any necessary changes. Does this ML work? Judge for yourself. In the first two weeks after I came on board, my client had to hire 10 full-time employees. And now, after the first six weeks, their sales are up 40%. You know, that's quite a lot when you consider they were already doing 14 million per year. I'd like to take full credit for all this, but I can't. In the first place, Sam and Leslie Fishbind know the furniture business inside and out, and Sam is truly a superb copywriter. By the way, all this seems to be working out so well, I think they're going to syndicate our advertising by making our collective expertise available to one other furniture dealer in each metro area throughout the United States. Back to the salt lines. Okay. So right now, I owe Sam and Leslie some themes and headlines, and I'm going to create them right here before your very eyes. What I'm going to do first is I'm going to loosen up my mind by reading some gag headlines written by Sam and his staff. I'm not doing this just for laughs. No, I'm doing it to get my mind in gear and see if I can catch a spark. Here are the headlines I'm looking at. Modern-day King Tut furnishes bizarre backyard burial tomb with Casey-fine furniture. I want only the best, he explains, because you can take it with you. Two-headed woman shops Casey and gets double whiplash looking at bargains. Pregnant woman has a cow when she sees her leather sofa for less at Casey's. Scientists reveal Elvis lives. Close friends confirm King's plan to redecorate Graceland with Casey-fine furniture. Vampires, duped with back pain, ditches coffin for Casey bedding in latest attempt to rest in peace. Siamese twins come unglued doing double-take at Casey blockbuster sale. Human cannonball blown away by Casey's explosive price cut and soaring selections and so on. This stuff is bizarre even by my standards, but it does serve to get the juices flowing, doesn't it? Now, let's review some of the actual headlines we've already used. Here are a few. Warning, don't hire any interior decorator until you hear this. Local furniture dealer will give away over $1 million in discounts on her entire inventory this July 4th weekend because of bulldozers tearing up her streets. Need more furniture? Five reasons why you should not shop at the store mentioned below. Extremely low prices from local furniture store may be deemed unfair to competition. Who else wants to buy a room for furniture without making payments for at least six months? Do you make these mistakes when you shop for furniture? Are you willing to walk up to three blocks to save as much as 50% on all the furniture you need? Local businessmen swears under oath he did not steal any of the furniture he is selling so cheaply. And so on. Okay, as promised a few pages ago, I am now going to reveal the simple way to manage all this data to come up with something new. First, I do what I just did, namely, I review what's already been done. Next, I get to my own personal collection of headline cards and pick them up and find them. I'm getting excited and look at them and think about them and flip them out onto the floor like I would a deck of cards. And I cogitate and I remember. I remember that someone once said with a justice learned at hand that freedom of speech does not give you the right to yell fire in a crowded theater. I remember my headline must not only grab attention, it must also be true and relevant. I remember that Casey Fine furniture really does have a problem with the street around their store being torn up. I remember that they really do have great furniture. Paulette and I are buying a whole household from them at extremely low prices. I remember, hey, I've got it. Check this out. The amazing secret of the local furniture dealer who is giving away all these free samples. See, what Sam and Leslie have is some really neat coffee table. They got such a deal on that they can afford to give them away to all new buyers and their amazing secret is that they discovered by offering a pre-premium to induce you to come in and buy and then giving rock bottom prices to keep you happy, you'll be a loyal customer forever and the competition won't stand a chance. And as usual, when Sam Fishbine does his usual brilliant job of fleshing out this idea, we'll have, I believe, another winner. And now that I've finished my newsletter for this month and developed another theme and headline for the Fishbines, I ask you, wasn't that a nifty way to kill two birds with one stone? Amen. Sincerely, Gary C. Halbert, The Hemmingway of Headlines. PPS. I just took on the marketing job of selling an album that contains the earliest Elvis Presley concert ever recorded. Wait till you see that ad. PPS. Damn, I'm good. And that is the end of the newsletter. And one thing I think you can see from that is just how very conversational Gary's newsletters are. As indeed his copy is, how very much it's like he's having a very personal conversation with you. And I think that's one of the main things you really get from reading these letters is the personalness of the letters, the amount of personal detail that he puts in here. You know so much, just from reading this one letter, you know so much about Gary. You know that he likes to fish. You know that he doesn't like to write. You see his method of working. It's filled with little insights into him. It's filled with little confessional asides. And all these things make you bond with him as a leader. They make you trust him. They make you want to buy things from him. They make you want to hire him to be a copywriter for you. Another thing I think is really, really key to Gary's writing that you see from this newsletter is just the way he loves sounds, the way he loves rhythms. You can hear just the simplicity of his writing. It's filled with simple sentences. It's filled with simple words. My head hurts. My feet stink. And I don't let Jimmy Swagger. I don't want to be here at my desk. Just virtually all single, single syllable words. Writing has a rhythm and a flow to it. The headlines that he gave as examples have a simple, almost like a high-cruel kind of a quality to them. I think the other thing to notice too, just from the point of view of how good Gary is at promoting himself, how much he talks about what a great copywriter he is, and he does it in a way that one finds endearing rather than as bragging. There's a couple of places in here. You'll notice where he has an opportunity and takes it to talk about the results of what he's done for the furniture store, for instance, and then he promotes one of his products and tells you that you should buy it and give the phone number. And I think that's such an important thing about Gary and such an important thing that a lot of writers, a lot of marketers don't do enough is just being willing to sell, just selling at every opportunity, and doing it in an endearing and friendly and almost like a tongue-in-cheek way rather than a heavy-handed way. Gary was so good at that. I love a lot of the advice that he gives in here, the advice about writing, where he tells you to write a lot. He brings up the example of the novelist that wrote ten novels. You know, more and more they're finding that talent is really not nearly so important as the amount of hours that you spend doing something. And when you look at people who seem to be such natural, inherent geniuses, people like the Beatles or Bach or Mozart or even certain writers, you find that they actually put in a tremendous amount of time honing their craft. They didn't just bring fully formed. And they did it by writing and writing or in the case of the Beatles playing and playing, the Beatles played in Germany for so many years and played. I don't know what was two gigs a day, every day of the week they'd be playing, but just thousands of hours total. So I think that's great advice. I think the advice about writing down the headline advice certainly about writing down the headlines from books like The Reader's Digest, from Cosmo, from any magazine, or from any play from the Enquirer, many plays that's good with headlines, just writing them down as you suggest is a great exercise because it really neurologically kind of gets them into your brain. And then using those headlines as a way to inspire you is just brilliant because just from having read them, I think the, well, they open your mind and get the pathways going. And of course you can also model your headlines on them, not by stealing or using them word for word, but just by being inspired by the concept of the headlines. Another thing I wanted to mention in that section where he does that analysis of the headline, at last scientists discover a new way to look younger in 17 days. I mean, that was just such a brilliant analysis, but I think it's really important to see how the mind of a great copywriter works. And the depth of, as Jay Abraham would say, the depth of forensic analysis that a copywriter like Gary does when he looks at a simple 13-word headline like that and really delves into every word and sees what it does. And that's the same kind of thinking Gary put into his own headlines that he wrote. One of the ways that Gary doesn't come off as being arrogant no matter how much he brags about himself is that he always seems to pull it back a little. One of the ways he does that here is by very generously giving Sam Fishbine a lot of credit for the advertising where he says, I'd like to take full credit for all this, but I can't. In the first place, Sam and Leslie Fishbine know the furniture business inside and out, and Sam is truly a superb copywriter. So I think that was a great way to kind of be very generous and give Sam credit. And at the same time, Gary is able to kind of position himself and take the edge off what otherwise could seem like a lot of bragging. There's a technique that Gary uses I think is really important, and that is he shows you when he's showing you how he uses those headlines that were Kit Kit that I guess the people at Sam Fishbine's place came up with with brainstorming the outrageous headlines. He shows how he uses them as pre-association to help him get started. And I think that's such a good way of getting unstuck. People just don't take advantage of, and that's to use material like that, to use any material that you can find that can get your brain going, that can make associations, and think, well, how can I use this? How can I be outrageous? What can I come up with that's based on this, that's inspired by that? And again, it's so great that Gary gave people such an insight into the way that he worked. Grewed the bad, the ugly, he really lets you in. And again, that was part of I think Gary's charm, the fascination that people had with Gary, is the way he generously revealed himself, what's in all, the way he generously revealed his writing practices, the way he was so unfettered in giving his opinion, even at the end here where he says, it's almost like he lets you in on a secret. And now that I've finished my newsletter for this month and developed another theme and headline for the Fishbinds, I ask you, wasn't this a nifty way to kill two birds with one stone? And it really was. It was a neat way to do the headline that he needed to do and get a newsletter out at the same time, and he tells you exactly what he's doing. I hope this gives you an idea of what could be learned just from one single issue of the Gary Halbert letter, and I've really just scratched the surface. So it's been great having the opportunity to do this with you. You've been listening to an original issue of the Gary Halbert letter. You can get more free, powerful, and timeless marketing advice in a mobile-friendly format by signing up at thegarryhalbertletter.com. Again, that's thegarryhalbertletter.com. See you there.
